item.title: Learning Golang from Python (II) - Project structure
item.description: In the previous post, I went into details on how to make golang work like python. After reading up a lot, I came to sense that the best way to learn golang is to unlearn how things are done in python and learn the way of Go. Workpace In python, I tend to have a nested workspace where I group various related modules together. ├── dist ├── private │ ├── app.

item.title: Learning Golang from Python (I) - understanding imports and packages
item.description: The first problem when trying to learn golang that I encountered is wrapping my head around how packages and import work in golang. Here is what I discovered and hope that others can benefit from it. Let’s start 3 files that we want to migrate from python to golang # hello_world.py import world import lib.adder print("Hello {0}".format(world.world())) print("1 + 1 = {0}".format(lib.adder.add(1, 1)))# world.py def world(): return "World"# lib/adder.py def add(x, y): return x + y File structure

item.title: Daily Git Workflow
item.description: I would like to share a workflow that I recently got used to when using git. When using git, there are a few ideas that people always talk about. Commit often Commit should be logical working chunk This workflow really embraces these ideas. Usually my day will start with me writing code for an hour or two. During this period, I will be heavily committing my code, usually after I finish a logical part.

item.title: Migration to hugo
item.description: I have decided to start doing a brain dump of random knowledge into blog posts instead of writing more structure tutorials which I can’t get around to finish. So to “celebrate” this decision, I have moved to hugo. It took me a day to migrate my 10+ pages from jekyll and modify the default theme to have the structure that I want. A lot of the pages are slightly outdated, so I will be updating them soon.

item.title: Accessing C++ objects from lua
item.description: In this tutorial, I will cover one method that you can use to access C++ objects from Lua. To start, let me describe the scenario that we will be implementing. Assume that we are making a game and we want to calculate the damage that each unit takes in a fight. Each unit has a damage attribute and a health attribute. We will do the calculation in the Lua script but store the attributes inside the C++ objects.

item.title: JPIO Query Guide
item.description: This document outlines the features that I want to have in JPIO and hopefully can get to implement them. We will use an example json and use it as a reference to see what each of the command do. Example Json { "books" : [ { "id" : ..., "name" : ..., "author" : <author_id>, }, { "id" : ..., "name" : ..., "author" : <author_id>, }, { "id" : .

item.title: Lua tutorial
item.description: Welcome to the Lua tutorial. This is not a tutorial about Lua as a programming language; There are plenty of those around. Instead, I will assume you know the basic of lua and how Objects are modeled in Lua. This tutorial will cover how to write C++ programs that interact with Lua. There are many reasons why you would want to do this but usually is to allow for extension and modification without recompilation.

item.title: Object-oriented in Lua
item.description: Coming soon…. Note: I probably will not be able to write this part, as I didn’t use Lua to create objects. If anyone have experience doing this, I can link to you =). Just let me know

item.title: Packaging lua functions
item.description: In part 2 of the tutorial, we have seen how we can call a Lua function from a C++ program. In this part of the tutorial, we will make use of that and put the functions in proper packages/modules. The main reason to do this is to make sure we don’t dirty the global namespaces, similar to why we use namespace in C++. First, let us look at the modification we do to the lua script.

item.title: Query Specification
item.description: Language Specification # Statements <Statement> :- (<Statement>) <Statement> :- <Statement>|<Statement> <Statement> :- <SelectionStatement><Selector> <Statement> :- <SelectionStatement>=<Statement> <Statement> :- <SelectionStatement><FunctionChain> # Functions <FunctionChain> :- <Function><FunctionChain> <FunctionChain> :- <Function> # Selectors <SelectionStatement> :- <Selector><SelectionStatement> <SelectionStatement> :- <Iterator><SelectionStatement> <SelectionStatement> :- <Iterator> <SelectionStatement> :- <Selector> <Selector> :- .<int> <Selector> :- .<float> <Selector> :- .<string> <Selector> :- .[<int>] # Iterators <Iterator> :- .[*] <Iterator> :- .[<int>:<int>] <Iterator> :- .[:<int>] <Iterator> :- .[<int>:] # Values <Value> :- <SelectionStatement> <Value> :- <int> <Value> :- <float> <Value> :- <string> <Value> :- <json> <string> :- s(.

item.title: Running C++ function from Lua
item.description: In this tutorial, I will cover how to run a C++ function from a Lua script. This is a pretty easy part so I am going to just show the code. extern "C" { static int compute(lua_State* L) { int x = luaL_checkint(L, 1); int y = luaL_checkint(L, 2); lua_pushnumber(L, x - y); return 1; } } int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { lua_State* L = setupLuaState(); lua_pushcfunction(L, compute); // push the c function to the stack lua_setglobal(L, "compute"); // add the function to the global load(L, "run.

item.title: Running Lua function from C++
item.description: In this tutorial, we will cover how to run a Lua function with a C++ program. There are 2 parts to the tutorial. The first part will cover how to call the function. The second part will show a simple way to wrap your Lua function inside a C++ function. In additional, we will see how to return multiple values from Lua function and how to access it from C++.

item.title: Setting Up
item.description: This tutorial is to set up the lua environment in our C++ program and make sure we can get it to run. Let me first show you the full code. /** * main.cpp */ #include <lua.hpp> #include <iostream> #include <vector> /** * A Simple function to load and run the file. Instead of just using luaL_dofile, I have split them up to print the proper error message. */ bool load(lua_State* L, const std::string& filename) { int status = luaL_loadfile(L, filename.

item.title: External Resources and links
item.description: Some useful links, just keeping them here for references. Git Good git branching model Concepts on git trees and reset Git internals Python Hitchhiker’s guide to python - A must read for new python developer. Beyond PEP8 - Very good talk about coding style. Python-Tornado Roughly how tornado async works My custom option parser for tornado

item.title: My Tooling and Workflow
item.description: History I only started working on the commandline around 2012, mainly because I need to edit python scripts on the server. At this stage, I didn’t really dive into vim plugins and stuff, I just used the default one. It was only until when I started making game in C++ that I started really upgrade my tooling and build my vimfiles and dotfiles. Around 2014, I started working more on the remote server and I added tmux to my tooling.
